segments.s301-g57: This is a temporal + locative fronting in classical/poetic Persian. بدین پنج روز (badīn panj rūz) functions as a temporal adverbial 'for these five days' — بدین is an archaic/poetic form (corresponding to modern در این/برای این) marking 'in/for these'. اندر این رزمگاه is the locative 'in/on this battlefield' (اندر = in/within, این = this, رزمگاه = battlefield). Putting time and place up front is common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s301-g58: همی is a classical iterative/continuous adverbial particle (marks repeated or ongoing action). کشته here functions as 'the slain' / past-participial noun used as object (i.e. 'slain ones' or 'slayings'), and جستند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of جستن used in an older/poetic sense with کشته to express 'caused to be slain' / 'slew'. ز ایران سپاه is a source/agent phrase 'the army from Iran' (ز = poetic 'from/of', ایران = Iran/Iranians, سپاه = army) placed after the verb, as is normal in classical word order.
segments.s301-g59: بشستند is past, 3rd-person plural ('they washed'); ایرانیان را is the definite/object-marked direct object ('the Iranians' with را marking them as the object). ز گرد here means 'from the dust' (گرد = dust), so the whole segment says they washed the Iranians free of dust — a ritual/cleaning after battle. Word order is verb + object + prepositional phrase (common in older Persian).
segments.s301-g60: سزاوار هر یک یکی دخمه کرد is a compact clause: سزاوار 'appropriate/fit' qualifies the action, هر یک 'each one', یکی 'one (a single)', دخمه 'tomb/sepulchre', and کرد is the past 3rd-person singular of کردن 'to make/do' (here 'he [or they] made'). The sense: 'for each one, as was fitting, he made one tomb.' Note the adjective سزاوار stands before the quantified phrase and کرد closes the clause.
segments.s301-g9: دمادم is an adverb meaning “constantly/continually” placed before the rest of the clause for emphasis. شما is the 2nd-person plural/formal pronoun (subject). از + پسم (پس + ـم) literally yields “from/behind me” (پس here = ‘after/behind’ with the possessive suffix -م). بگذرید is a 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive/imperative form of گذریدن (گذر + -ید), meaning “you pass” or “pass by”; the phrase as a whole reads “you constantly pass behind me.”
segments.s301-g10: به marks direction/destination (“toward/to the Jihun”). جیحون is the Amu Darya (proper name). و links the coordinated nouns. زورق زمان is a noun phrase where زورق (“small boat/skiff”) is qualified by زمان (“time”); this is a poetic compound (“skiff of Time”). مشمرید is an archaic/obscure verb form (not common in modern Persian) that here functions as a 2nd-person plural finite verb meaning roughly “you sail/are borne” (the exact nuance is archaic).
segments.s301-g11: شب تیره is a noun + adjective phrase (night + dark/ominous). با introduces an accompanying agent/instrument (“with”): با لشکر افراسیاب = “with Afrasiab’s army,” indicating who accompanies the night (poetic way to say ‘during the dark night Afrasiab’s army ...’). Word order (noun + adjective, and with-phrase after) is typical of Persian poetic phrasing.
segments.s301-g12: گذر کرد is a compound/periphrastic past: گذر (pass/passage) + کرد (do) = ‘passed’ (he/they passed). از آموی is the prepositional phrase ‘from the Amu’ (آموی = the Amu/Amu Darya here). و + بگذاشت begins a coordinated clause: بگذاشت is past tense (بـ + گذاشت) meaning literally ‘he left/put’ or ‘he let [it be]’; بگذاشت آب here reads idiomatically as ‘left the water (behind)’—i.e. they crossed and left the river behind them. آب is the direct object ‘water.’
segments.s301-g13: همه روی کشور is an idiomatic locative phrase meaning ‘over the whole face/surface of the country’ (همه = all, روی here = ‘upon/over’). به introduces the directional/locational pair that follows: به بی‌راه و راه = ‘on/off the road(s),’ with بی‌راه (‘off the road/wrong way’) and راه (‘road’) coordinated by و. The structure states where, across the realm, the following items are found.
segments.s301-g14: سراپرده و خیمه are coordinated nouns (‘pavilions and tents’). بد is an adjective meaning ‘bad/ill/unfavorable’ describing those shelters; it precedes the following prepositional/privative phrase بی سپاه (‘without an army’), where بی = ‘without’ and سپاه = ‘army/troops.’ Grammatically this string lists what is present across the land and qualifies their poor state and lack of troops.
segments.s301-g15: سپیده = ‘dawn/first light.’ چو is the conjunction ‘when’ (here introducing a temporal clause). از باختر indicates source/direction ‘from the west.’ بردمید is an archaic/poetic past-tense verb meaning ‘rose/lifted up’ (بردمیدن used poetically for rising or lifting); together, سپیده چو از باختر بردمید = ‘when dawn rose from the west.’
segments.s301-g16: طلایه سپه is a noun phrase ‘the vanguard of the army’ (طلایه = vanguard, سپه = host/army). را is the definite-object marker but in poetry can also highlight the phrase; here it marks the phrase that follows the verb. به هامون uses به as locative/toward and هامون = ‘the plain’ (place). ندید is the negative past of دیدن (ن + دید = ‘did not see’). The clause thus says he did not see the army’s vanguard on the plain.
segments.s301-g17: بیامد is the past 3rd‑person singular of آمدن (he/it came) and opens the clause; the prepositional phrase به مژده ('with/to the tidings') marks the content/quality of the arrival (i.e. he came bearing good news). بر شهریار places the destination/recipient ('to the king') after the news phrase; بر here functions as a preposition 'to/toward' used with شهریار.
segments.s301-g18: که introduces a content (that-) clause giving the news. پردخته شد is a pas­sive/inchoative verbal construction: پردخته is a participial/adjectival form and شد functions as the auxiliary 'was/was made' (here giving the sense 'had been X'); شاه is the referent (the king), and ز این کارزار means 'from/after this battle' with ز as the poetic preposition 'from'.
segments.s301-g19: همه دشت خیمه‌ست is a verbless/predicative clause meaning 'the whole plain is tents/a camp': همه modifies دشت ('the entire plain'), and خیمه‌ست ('is tents' / 'is an encampment') is a nominal predicate. و پرده‌سرای links a second nominal predicate (pavilion‑place/scene) with the coordinating و.
segments.s301-g20: ز دشمن سواری is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of/from enemy horsemen' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register), followed by the verb نبینم which is 1st‑person singular negative present/subjunctive used narratively as 'I see not / I do not see'. به جای is an adverbial phrase 'in place' / 'anywhere' with به marking the locative relation to جای.
segments.s301-g21: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'); خسرو is the subject. دوان شد is a compound where دوان (root meaning 'run, rush') plus شد (here inchoative/auxiliary 'became') yields 'he began to run' or 'he rushed off'. به خاک indicates direction/manner 'to the ground' or 'down to the earth'.
segments.s301-g22: نیایش کنان is a circumstantial participial phrase: نیایش ('prayer') with the gerundial/participle کنان ('doing/uttering') gives 'praying' or 'with prayer'. پیش یزدان پاک is the prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the pure God' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', یزدان پاک = 'the pure God').
segments.s301-g23: همی گفت is an iterative/imperfective past (همی before a verb indicates continuous or repeated action in older Persian) meaning 'he was saying/kept saying'. کای is the vocative particle 'O', addressing the addressee; روشن کردگار is a series of epithets: روشن ('luminous/illustrious') modifying کردگار ('Creator/God'). Note: the lemma senses provided for گفت are nouns (conversation), so the verb sense must be treated separately.
segments.s301-g24: This segment strings three epithets with coordinating و: جهاندار ('holder/ruler of the world') و بیدار ('and awake/alert') و پروردگار ('and Lord/Nurturer'). They continue the vocative description begun in the previous segment.
segments.s301-g25: This is a 2nd-person past clause: subject 'تو' + past verb 'دادی' (you gave). The enclitic object 'م' (m-) with the object marker 'را' marks 'me' as the definite object (classical spelling split here). A coordinated list of direct objects follows ('فر و دیهیم و زور') joined by و; 'فر' is the technical term farr (divine/royal glory), 'دیهیم' is an older/poetic word for the folk/people or the rural populace, and 'زور' is 'strength/might'.
segments.s301-g26: 'تو کردی' is the 2nd-person past of کردن used causatively meaning 'you made/caused'. The phrase 'دل و چشم' is a coordinated object 'heart and eye', and 'بدخواه' qualifies them as belonging to the ill-wisher ('of the ill-wisher'); the adjective 'کور' (= blind) follows the nouns and is the predicate result: 'you made the ill-wisher’s heart and eye blind.'
segments.s301-g27: Imperative clause: the prepositional particle 'ز' (from/of) introduces 'گیتی' (the world), then the direct object 'ستمکاره را' ('the oppressor' + object marker 'را'). The verbal phrase 'دور کن' is an imperative 'make [him] distant / drive away.' In classical poetic order the prepositional phrase appears before the object and verb.
segments.s301-g28: Another imperative: 'ز بیمش' (by/from his fear) gives the cause or instrument; 'همه‌ساله' is an adverbial 'every year', and 'رنجور کن' is the imperative 'make [him] distressed / afflicted'. The sense is: cause him to be worn/distressed by fear year after year.
segments.s301-g29: 'چو' is the subordinating particle 'when'; subject 'خورشید' (the sun) modified by 'زرین' (golden). 'سپر' (shield) is the object, and 'برگرفت' is the simple past 'took up/raised' (بر + گرفت) with the sun as implied subject: 'when the golden sun took up its shield.'
segments.s301-g30: Subject 'شب' (the night) performs the action expressed by final verb 'گرفت' (took). 'آن شعر پیروزه' is the thing taken: 'that poem/verse' qualified by 'پیروزه' (here best read as 'victorious' or triumphant). The preposition 'بر' + 'سر' yields 'on the head' → 'the victorious verse crowned the night' (literary personification: the night is crowned).
segments.s301-g31: جهاندار is the subject (the ruler) followed by the past-tense verb بنشست (3rd person singular) 'sat down'; the locative phrase بر تخت عاج 'on the ivory throne' follows the verb. تخت عاج is a noun + modifier sequence meaning 'ivory throne' (عاج qualifying تخت). This word order (Subject + Past Verb + locative phrase) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s301-g32: This clause means 'he placed ... upon his head.' به سر is the prepositional object 'on/onto the head' and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed' (3rd person singular); the particle بر appears as a directional prefix/adverbial 'upon' combined with the verb (بر نهاد) — older/poetic Persian often uses both به and بر together to mark the target and the direction. آن دل‌افروز تاج is a noun phrase 'that heart-delighting crown' with the adjective دل‌افروز following the noun تاج, and the demonstrative آن 'that/the' specifying it; the subject is omitted but understood from the previous clause.
segments.s301-g1: چو marks a temporal clause here meaning “when.” نیمی ز تیره شب is a genitive phrase “half of the dark night” (نیمی = “half” placed before ز + noun). اندر گذشت is the past verb phrase “had passed,” with اندر (in/within) placed before the verb in poetic word order; گذشت is simple past.
segments.s301-g2: سپهر is the subject “the heavens/sky.” از بر کوه is a prepositional phrase “from/at the side of the mountain” (poetic double preposition از + بر). ساکن بگشت is a predicate in which ساکن (still, motionless) combines with a past verb گشت (with the prefixed ب- appearing in classical/poetic forms) to mean “became/turned still.”
segments.s301-g3: سپهدار ترکان is a genitival noun phrase “the commander of the Turks” (title + modifier). بنه بر نهاد is a verbal phrase where نهاد (past of ‘to put/place’) is the main verb; بنه (here a noun meaning a heap/camp or bundle) is placed before the verb (typical Persian object order) and بر is the preposition “on/over.”
segments.s301-g4: سپه را is the marked (را) recipient/object “the army” (in Persian the recipient can be marked by را). همه modifies the following nouns “all/entire.” ترگ و جوشن are two coordinated nouns “rags and cuirasses/armor.” بداد is a past verb meaning ‘gave/supplied’ in classical usage (پیش‌واره ب- + دادن), here meaning he provided these items to the army.
segments.s301-g5: طلایه is the noun “vanguard/forefront.” بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb meaning “he commanded/ordered/said” (classical form with b-). تا ده هزار is a numeric extent phrase “up to/numbering ten thousand” where تا expresses the extent/limit.
segments.s301-g6: بود is the past existential/copular “there were/was.” ترک is the subject “Turks.” برگستوان here functions as a verbal/participial descriptor (from بازگشت/گشتن) meaning “returned/advanced/appeared” in classical/poetic usage. ور is an archaic conjunction (here with sense ‘again/and’), and سوار is a predicate noun/adjective “mounted/horsemen.” Together the clause asserts the presence of Turks as mounted riders.
segments.s301-g7: چنین is an adverb “thus/so.” گفت here is the verb “he said” (classical past); با لشکر افراسیاب is an adjunct meaning “to/with Afrasiab’s army” (با + لشکر, name in genitive position). Word order places the manner/adverb first, then the verb and the company/recipient.
segments.s301-g8: که introduces a subordinate clause (that/introducing reported speech). من is the first-person pronoun “I.” چون here is an interrogative adverb “how.” گذر is the noun “crossing/passage,” and یابم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive/imperfect form of ‘to find/attain’ used in indirect question sense (“may I find / can I find”). از رود آب is the prepositional phrase “across/from the river water” (poetic doubling of رود + آب).
segments.s301-g41: بدیشان (to them) is a prepositional/object-like dative placed before the verb; چنین (thus) is an adverb modifying گفت. گفت is the past verb 'said' (poetic word-order places the verb before its subject here). بیدار شاه is the subject phrase 'the awake king' placed after the verb for stylistic inversion.
segments.s301-g42: که‌ای is the vocative/exclamatory address (classical 'که ای' = 'O'), calling out to the addressees. نامداران modifies سپاه (the host) and ایران is a genitive qualifier placed before or after the head in poetic order: 'the renowned ones, the army of Iran' — a vocative phrase addressing the army's chiefs.
segments.s301-g43: چو here introduces a conditional/temporal clause ('if/when'). دشمن بود is a clause ('an enemy was / the enemy was present'); شاه را is the definite-object-marked 'the king' and کشته is a past participle 'killed' used predicatively (so 'had killed the king'). به at the end is a poetic clitic/particle linking the clause to what follows (in older Persian it can mark agency or simply connect clauses).
segments.s301-g44: گر is another conditional 'if'; آواره is the person 'a wanderer/displaced one' as the subject; از جنگ برگشته is a participial phrase 'returned from the war' (برگشته = returned). The final به again functions as a connective/poetic particle tying this alternative condition to the previous clause.
segments.s301-g45: چو functions as 'when'; پیروزگر is the agent 'the conqueror/victor'. دادمان is a past verb with the first-person plural clitic ('gave to us' / 'bestowed on us'). فرهی is an epithet or object meaning 'noble'/'of nobility' describing what was given (here used as a substantive).
segments.s301-g46: بزرگی و دیهیم form a coordinate noun phrase 'greatness and ...' that qualifies or names what was bestowed; شاهنشهی (شاهنشاهی) is the domain/possessive idea 'of kingship/imperial rule' describing the sphere of that greatness. Note دیهیم is an older/poetic word whose precise shade is archaic here but belongs grammatically with بزرگی as a paired quality of the kingship.
segments.s301-g47: ز گیتی is a prepositional/poetic phrase 'from the world' or 'throughout the world'. ستایش is the noun 'praise' and مر is the older/poetic form of مرا ('for me' / 'to me'). او را is the definite object 'him'; کنید is the plural imperative 'do / perform' — together: 'Offer him praise for me.'
segments.s301-g48: شب آید is a temporal subordinate clause 'when night comes' (آید = 'comes' / present/future sense). نیایش is 'prayer' and again مر او را is 'for him, for me' with را marking the direct object; کنید is the imperative plural 'perform/do'. The whole line instructs: 'When night comes, offer prayer for him on my behalf.'
segments.s301-g33: نیایش کنان is an adverbial participial phrase: نیایش (noun) + کنان (participle) = 'praying / offering supplication'. پیش او شد سپاه is a poetic word order where the verb شد ('became/arrived') follows the locative پیش او ('before him/in his presence') and the subject سپاه ('the host/army') appears last — together meaning 'the army came before him.'
segments.s301-g34: که begins a reported/wished clause introduced by their supplication. جاوید باد is an optative formula: جاوید 'eternal' + باد (optative particle) = 'may ... be eternal.' این سزاوار گاه is the noun phrase 'this deserving/fitting hour,' the thing wished to be eternal.
segments.s301-g35: شد is the past/resultative verb 'became' and governs the predicate بی‌نیاز ('free from need'). این لشکر is the subject ('this army/host'), and از خواسته ('from desire/want') is an ablative complement specifying what they became free of.
segments.s301-g36: که introduces an explanatory/circumstantial clause ('for/because/that'). از لشکر شاه چین names the party ('from the army of the King of China' — شاه چین = 'King of China' / 'the Chinese king's army'). ماند باز is a compound idea 'remained behind/was left' (ماند = 'remained'; باز here emphasizes being left/available/in reserve).
segments.s301-g37: همی گفت is a habitual/continuous narrative phrase meaning 'kept saying' or 'was saying' (همی marking continuous aspect). هر کس 'everyone' is the subject, and اینت فسوس is the object phrase 'this whisper/idle talk' (اینت a dialectal/demonstrative 'this', فسوس = idle/insidious talk).
segments.s301-g38: که here introduces the content of the talk ('that ...'). او رفت is a simple past clause 'he went/has gone.' با + list (لشکر و بوق و کوس) marks accompaniment and martial instruments: 'with the army and trumpet and drum.' The conjunction و simply links the items.
segments.s301-g39: شب تیره is a nominal phrase 'the dark night' (figurative for a dire time). از دست here means 'by the hand/agency of' (از + دست + agent) and points to the doers; پرمایگان is the agent 'the noblemen / great ones.'
segments.s301-g40: بشد is the past/resultative 'it became / it happened' (narrative 'became'). نامداری is the resultant noun 'renown / fame.' چنین رایگان is an adverbial phrase 'thus, freely / without cost,' qualifying how renown came about.
segments.s301-g49: This clause literally orders its pieces as Object (آن را) + relative 'that/which' (که) + verb (خواهد کند) + subject (شوربخت). «آن را که خواهد کند» = "that which he wants/does," with «خواهد کند» a periphrastic present (will/wants + does) meaning "wishes and does." The final noun «شوربخت» is the subject (the wretch) placed after the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s301-g50: «یکی بی‌هنر برنشاند به تخت» = "(they) seat/put a certain unskilled person on the throne." «یکی» = "someone/a certain one;" «بی‌هنر» is an adjective "unskilled." «برنشاند» (past/causative form) means "place/seat (on)," and the preposition «به» marks the destination (to the throne). Word order is Subject omitted (impersonal/they) + object phrase.
segments.s301-g51: «از این کوشش و پرسشت رای نیست» literally "from this effort and your questioning there is no opinion/advice." «از» introduces source/ground, «این کوشش و پرسشت» is the coordinated noun phrase (note «پرسشت» = "your question/asking," second-person suffix), and «رای نیست» = "there is no counsel/decision."
segments.s301-g52: «که» here gives the reason: "because/for." «با داد او» = "by/under his justice" (با = by/with, داد = justice, او = his). «بنده را پای نیست» is literal "the servant has no foot," an idiom meaning "the servant has no standing/position"; «را» marks the definite object/topic (emphatic), and «نیست» is the negation "is not/does not exist."
segments.s301-g53: «بباشم بدین رزمگه پنج روز» = "I shall be/remain in this battlefield five days." «بباشم» is first-person singular form of "to be/remain" (here future/intentional). «بدین رزمگه» = "in/at this battlefield" (بدین = به + این, locative), and «پنج روز» is a duration (five days). Note: the segment is poetic; «بدین» is one unit meaning "in this."
segments.s301-g54: «ششم روز هرمزد گیتی فروز» places a temporal phrase then an epithet: "on the sixth day, Hurmuz (Ahura Mazda), the world's-illuminator." «ششم روز» = "sixth day" (ordinal before noun), and «هرمزد گیتی فروز» is a two-word epithet naming the deity and qualifying him as 'world-brightener.'
segments.s301-g55: «برآید برانیم ز ایدر سپاه» is a pair of verbs followed by the source and object: «برآید» (jussive/optative or impersonal present) "let it/let him arise/come forth," «برانیم» = "we will/shall drive (away)," and «ز ایدر سپاه» = "the army from here" (ز = from, ایدر = here, سپاه = army). The clause expresses: "Let them come out from here and we will drive the army off."
segments.s301-g56: «که» gives the reason: "because." «او کین فزایست» = "he increases hatred/vengeance" (کین subject, فزایست = "it increases" — an older third-person form). «و ما کینه خواه» = "and we desire/seek revenge" (خواه = "want/seek" used as main verb). Together: "for he stokes hatred and we are bent on vengeance."

sentences.s301-s29: For five days on this battlefield the Iranian army kept slaying (their foes).
sentences.s301-s30: They washed the Iranians of the dust; for each one fittingly they made a single tomb.
sentences.s301-s5: All the while you pass behind me toward the Jihun, and you sail the skiff of Time.
sentences.s301-s6: On a dark night Afrasiab's host crossed the Amu and left the water behind.
sentences.s301-s7: Throughout the whole realm, along both off-paths and roads, pavilions and tents—ill arranged and without troops.
sentences.s301-s8: When dawn rose from the west, he did not see the vanguard of the host on the plain.
sentences.s301-s9: A messenger came to the king with the glad tidings that the king had been delivered from this battle.
sentences.s301-s10: The whole plain is a camp of tents and pavilions; I see no enemy horseman anywhere.
sentences.s301-s11: When Khosrow heard, he ran to the ground, praying before the pure God.
sentences.s301-s12: He kept saying, 'O luminous Creator, ruler of the world, Awake and Lord.'
sentences.s301-s13: You gave me farr (divine glory), the people/folk, and might; you made the ill-wisher’s heart and eye blind.
sentences.s301-s14: Drive the tyrant away from the world; let him be made afflicted year after year by his fear.
sentences.s301-s15: When the golden sun lifted its shield, that victorious verse crowned the night.
sentences.s301-s16: The sovereign sat on the ivory throne and set that heart-delighting crown upon his head.
sentences.s301-s1: When half of the dark night had passed, the heavens beside the mountain grew still.
sentences.s301-s2: The commander of the Turks set up the camp, and he supplied the whole army with rags and cuirasses.
sentences.s301-s3: He commanded a vanguard of up to ten thousand; there were Turks as mounted horsemen.
sentences.s301-s4: Thus Afrasiab said to his army: 'How shall I find a crossing of the river?'
sentences.s301-s21: The awake king said to them thus: "O renowned warriors of Iran's host!"
sentences.s301-s22: If the enemy has been present and has killed the king, or if a wanderer has returned from the battle,
sentences.s301-s23: When the conqueror bestowed upon us noble gifts — the greatness and the majesty of imperial rule,
sentences.s301-s24: From the whole world, offer praise for him to me; when night comes, offer for him prayer for me.
sentences.s301-s17: The host came before him, offering prayers, saying, “May this fitting hour be eternal.”
sentences.s301-s18: This host became free from want, for the Shah of China’s army remained behind.
sentences.s301-s19: Everyone kept saying that idle whisper: that he had gone with the army, with trumpet and drum.
sentences.s301-s20: By the hands (agency) of the nobles the dark night was turned into renown, thus freely.
sentences.s301-s25: The wretch does whatever he wishes; they seat an unskilled man on the throne.
sentences.s301-s26: From this effort and your questioning there is no counsel, for under his justice a servant has no standing.
sentences.s301-s27: I will remain in this battlefield five days; on the sixth day, Ahura Mazda, the world's illuminator,
sentences.s301-s28: Let the army come forth from here and we will drive them off, for he increases hatred and we seek vengeance.
